coredata.dc:title: Promising carbon nanosheet-based supercapacitor electrode materials from low-grade coals
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Tonkeswar
Chauhan, Himani
Deka, Sasanka
Chaudhary, Shanky
Boruah, Ratan
Saikia, Binoy K.
coredata.dc:description: The abundantly available low-grade coals were efficiently used to produce supercapacitor electrode materials by using wet-chemical methods. The low-grade coals were first chemically converted to graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs), and then their electrochemical parameters were extensively investigated to know their basic supercapacitor properties. The formation of the GCNSs was established by using scanning electron microscopy-energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDX), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) techniques. The carbon flakes of the size range of 2–50 μm were found to present in the coal-derived carbon nanomaterials. HRTEM analysis revealed the formation of layered GCNSs along with micropores/mesopores during the oxidative processes. The thermally stabled coal-derived GCNSs were found as promising electrode materials for supercapacitor demonstrating novel charge storage characteristics. The specific capacitance value of ∼125, 84 and 95 F g−1 with high rate capability over 1000 cycles were observed in an acidic electrolyte (H2SO4 1M) with considerably high power density and energy density.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Low-grade coal
Chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets
Chemical methods
Energy storage
Supercapacitors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets
Chemical characterizations of the raw coal samples and coal-derived GCNSs
Electrochemical study of the coal-derived GCNSs
Preparation of cell for two electrode system
Chemical characteristics of coals and coal-derived GCNSs
Electron beam analysis (SEM and TEM/HRTEM) of chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs)
FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis
Electrochemical and supercapacitor study of the coal-derived GCNSs
Factors affecting the specific capacitance of the GCNSs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM/HRTEM images of the coal-derived GCNSs: (a) Zoom-in view at the 20 nm scale of Coal-NK-S1; (b) HRTEM image of Coal-NK-S1 at 5 nm scale; (c)SAED pattern of Coal-NK-S1. The TEM/HRTEM images of the Coal-NK-S1 is reproduced from our previous study [34]. (d)overall view at the 50 nm scale of Coal-NK-S2; (e) HRTEM images of Coal-NK-S2 at 20 nm scale; (f)SAED pattern of Coal-NK-S2. (g)Zoom-in view at the 50 nm scale of Coal-T60-S2; (h) HRTEM images of Coal-T60-S2 at 5 nm scale; (i)SAED pattern of Coal-T60-S2. The cluster of chemically converted graphene-like nanosheets is seen along with micropores/mesopores.

(a) XRD patterns of the coal-derived GCNSs: Coal-NK-S1 (black), Coal-NK-S2 (red), Coal-T60-S2 (blue). One broad peak centered at around 27°, which may be due to the presence of some graphite-like structure embedded with amorphous carbon matrix and the introduction of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. (b) Raman spectra of Coal-NK-S1 (red), Coal-NK-S2 (black), Coal-T60-S2 (blue). The characteristic G-band for sp2-carbon and D-band for sp3-carbon is observed. (c) FTIR spectra of Coal-NK-S1 (black), Coal-NK-S2 (red), and Coal-T60-S2 (blue) representing that GCNSs contain abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and sp2 aromatic units.(d)XPS survey spectrum of Coal-NK-S1;(e)high-resolution deconvoluted C1s spectrum ofCoal-NK-S1; (f)high-resolution deconvoluted O1s spectrum of Coal-NK-S1. The C1S XPS peak splits into four main peaks of carbon species after oxidation, which are located at 284.13 eV (C=C) (black), 284.92 eV (C=C-C) (red), 285.71 eV (C–C) (blue), and 286.83 eV (C–O) (green). The XRD, FTIR, Raman, and XPS analysis of Coal-NK-S1 is reproduced from our previous study [34]. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Thermal analysis (TGA-DTA) of the coal-derived GCNSs: TGA (black), DTG (red), and DTA (blue) of (a)Coal-NK-S1 (reproduced from our previous study [34]; (b)Coal-NK-S2; (c)Coal-T60-S2. The thermal stability of the Coal-NK-S1 is found to be less in comparison to that of Coal-NK-S2 and Coal-T60-S2, which may be due to the presence of high amount of oxygen-containing functional groups. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Nyqustic plot of all three cells, Cell#1, Cell#2 and Cell#3 at 10 mHz to 100 kHz. (b) Nyqist plot at lower frequency range for all three cells indicating bulk (RB) and charge transfer resistance (RCT).
Bods plot calculated from electrochemical impedance data for (a) Cell#1, (b) Cell#2, and (c) Cell#3 respectively.

Cyclicvoltammogram (CV) studies of Cell#1, Cell#2 and Cell#3. (a) CV of Cell #1 in potential window of −0.4 V to 0.6 V at 10, 20, 50 and 100 mV s−1 (b and c) CV of Cell #2 and Cell#3 within potential window of 0–1.0 V at a scan rate of 10, 20, 50 and 100 mV s−1 (d) CV curve of all three cells at their specific potential window at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1.

Galvanostatic charge discharge study for all cells. (a–c) are charge-discharge cycles at 0.5 A g−1 cm−2 for Cell#1, Cell#2 and Cell#3 respectively (inset: few cycles to show magnified charge-dischargebehaviour of cells). (d) The plot of specific capacitance vs. a number of cycles.

(a–c) Charge-discharge patterns at different current from 3 A g−1 cm−2 to 0.5 A g−1 cm−2 for Ragon plot (power density vs energy density). (d) Ragon plots at current density 3 A g−1 cm−2 to 0.5 A g−1 cm−2 for all three cells.

(a) Comparison of single charge-discharge cycles for all three cells at current rate of 0.5 mA within their respective potential window. CV cycling curves for typical Cell#1, Cell#2 and Cell#3 at scan rate of 50 mV s−1 measured for 1000 cycles.
Flow diagram for the synthesis of chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs).
Summary of physico-chemical characteristics of the raw coal and coal-derived carbon materials (as received, wt. %).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: M: Moisture; Ash: Ash content; VM: Volatile Matter; FC: Fixed Carbon; C: Carbon; H: hydrogen; N: Nitrogen; TS: Total Sulfur; O:Oxygen (by difference). Physico-chemical characteristic of Coal-NK and Coal-NK-S1 is reproduced from our previous study [34].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calculated specific capacitance, ESR, energy density and power density for three different cells using GCNSs.

head.ce:title: Promising carbon nanosheet-based supercapacitor electrode materials from low-grade coals

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Petroleum and Coal Chemistry Group, Materials Science and Technology Division, CSIR-North East Institute of Science & Technology, Jorhat 785006, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Polymer Petroleum and Coal Chemistry Group
CSIR-North East Institute of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Delhi, North Campus, Delhi 110007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tezpur University, Tezpur 784001, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The abundantly available low-grade coals were efficiently used to produce supercapacitor electrode materials by using wet-chemical methods. The low-grade coals were first chemically converted to graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs), and then their electrochemical parameters were extensively investigated to know their basic supercapacitor properties. The formation of the GCNSs was established by using scanning electron microscopy-energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDX), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) techniques. The carbon flakes of the size range of 2–50 μm were found to present in the coal-derived carbon nanomaterials. HRTEM analysis revealed the formation of layered GCNSs along with micropores/mesopores during the oxidative processes. The thermally stabled coal-derived GCNSs were found as promising electrode materials for supercapacitor demonstrating novel charge storage characteristics. The specific capacitance value of ∼125, 84 and 95 F g−1 with high rate capability over 1000 cycles were observed in an acidic electrolyte (H2SO4 1M) with considerably high power density and energy density.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid increase in global energy consumption and the environmental impact of traditional energy resources lead to serious challenges to human health, energy security, and the environment; and reveal a growing need to develop new types of clean and sustainable energy conversion and storage systems, such as batteries and supercapacitors for electric vehicles with low exhaust emissions [1–4]. Supercapacitors are energy storage devices and have received tremendous attention because of their high power density, short charging time, and long cycling life [5,6], which play an important role in achieving better fuel economy, decreasing harmful emissions, and reducing the reliance on petroleum sources [1]. The world market for supercapacitors has been growing rapidly. Nanotechnology has opened up new frontiers by offering unique invention and new materials for energy storage [1]. Recently, different allotropes and derivatives of carbon such as activated carbons [7,8], carbon fiber [9,10], onion-like carbons [11], graphene oxide [12], carbon nanotubes [13–16], carbide-derived carbons [17], humic acid [18–20], and graphene [21,22], and their composites [23,24] have attracted much attention as a promising material in the charge storing. Despite the variety of high energy density supercapacitor carbons developed, their practical application is limited due to high production cost [25]. Hence, there is a substantial demand for demonstrating practical techniques for the selective large-scale and simple synthesis of high purity supercapacitor carbon materials from easily available and cheap materials.

At this point, high purity coal-derived carbon nanomaterials may be a viable source to develop low-cost supercapacitor carbon-materials [25]. Coal is the abundant source of carbon and being considered as feedstock for carbon-based materials. Several researchers have published descriptions for the preparation of coal-derived carbon nanomaterials by using different methods [26–30] but the literature on the electrochemical behavior of the coal-derived carbon nanomaterials is sparse. Considering all these aspects, an attempt has been made to utilize the low-grade coals as a best precursor of carbon for the preparation of typical carbon nanomaterials for using as supercapacitor material. The novelty of the present investigation is that the carbon source is from low-grade high sulfur coal which is typically different and cheap from other part of the country. As compared to the pure sp2-carbon such as graphite and high grade coal, the exfoliation of the small graphite-like crystalline domains that are inherent in low-grade coal is reported to be easy [27,29,30].

Here, we report the synthesis and detailed characterization of coal-derived chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs) via two typical wet-chemical oxidative methods followed by an extensive electrochemical study of this new carbon material.

The raw low-grade sub-bituminous coal samples (denoted as Coal-NK and Coal-T60) were obtained from Northeastern (NER) coalfields (India). The coals have high-sulfur contents (2–10%), where 75–90% of the sulfur is organically bound while the rest is in inorganic form viz. sulfate sulfur and pyritic sulfur [31,32]. Thus, the quality/grade of these NER Indian coals is low and needs to be upgraded for gainful utilization. The coal samples (∼1 kg each) were first air-dried, crushed below 3.180-mm sizes, and then sieved to < 0.211-mm sizes by using standard methods [33].H2SO4 (95–98%, Sigma-Aldrich), and HNO3 (70%, Sigma-Aldrich), NaOH (Merck) were used as received. Polytetrafluoroethylene membranes (Sartorius, lot number 11806-47-N) and dialysis bags (Sigma-Aldrich, dialysis tubing-cellulose membrane; average flat width 76 mm; D9402-100FT) were used to purify the carbon-materials prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Two typical reactions methods (method-1 and method-2) were used to prepare the chemically converted graphene-like nanosheets from these low-grade coals. The synthetic methods mentioned as method-1 is reported in our previous study [34]. Briefly, the raw coal sample (Coal-NK) was oxidized in the presence of HNO3 (98%) at 150 °C for 2 h at reflux condition. The oxidized coal sample was further treated with 1N NaOH and then acidified with 6N HCl acid to pH 1–1.5 to re-precipitate and maintained for 24 h. The acid-insoluble portions were concentrated by centrifugation, the supernatant was decanted, and the precipitates were washed with dilute HCl and ultrapure water until the filtrate become neutral. The precipitate was recovered, and freeze-dried to obtain the solid products. The final coal-derived carbon nanomaterial was denoted as Coal-NK-S1.

In the method-2, both the coal samples (Coal-NK and Coal-T60) were oxidized in the presence of a mixture of H2SO4 and HNO3 (1:1) for 24 h at reflux condition. After thermal oxidation (at about 150 °C), a brown-red solution resulted. The solution was then cooled in an ice-water bath and slowly poured into a beaker containing crushed ice. After that, the solution was dialyzed in a 1000 Da dialysis tube using distilled water. After dialysis was complete, the suspensions were centrifuged and collected. After purification by dialysis, the deposit was evaporated to dryness, washed with ethanol and acetone, and dried to collect the final solid product. The final coal-derived carbon nanomaterials (GCNSs) were denoted as Coal-NK-S2 and Coal-T60-S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical characterizations of the raw coal samples and coal-derived GCNSs
ce:section.ce:para: The proximate analysis and ultimate analysis (CHNS) of the samples were carried out in the Thermogravimetric Analyzer (Leco TGA701) [35] and Truspec CHN Macro Determinator (Leco: 630-100-300) [36], respectively. Sulfur was determined by using a Dual Range Sulfur Analyzer (Leco S-144DR: accuracy ±0.02) [37] and oxygen content was calculated by difference. For scanning electron microscopic (SEM) analysis, the samples were critical point dried, fixed to metal stubs with carbon conducting double-sided adhesive tape. The samples were then coated with platinum with the aid of an Auto Fine Coater (JFC-1600; JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) and examined under a scanning electron microscope (Model No. JSM-6390LV; JEOL). Observations on the SEM images were made at 20 kV with a working distance of 11 mm and spot size 36 mm. TEM images were taken by using a High Resolution-Transmittance Electron Microscope (HR-TEM; Joel JEM-2100, resolution: 1.9 Å to 1.4 Å, accelerating voltage: 60–200 KV in 50 V steps). The SEM and HR-TEM photomicrographs were further developed by using the “Image J” program (software version 1.47). AFM measurement was performed by using Nanomagnetics hpAFM. X-ray diffraction spectra were obtained using an X-ray powder Diffractometer (type: JDX-11P3A, JEOL: Japan). X-ray diffraction data of the samples were obtained with the starting angle of 2.00°, a stoping angle of 75.00°, and a step size of 0.05° with a scanning rate of 1° per minute with a Co (λ = 1.7902 Å) beam. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded in transmittance mode with 4 cm−1 spectral resolution using FT-IR spectrophotometer (IR Affinity-1, Shimadzu, Japan) and IR solution software. The Raman analysis was performed on a Laser micro-Raman system (Make: Horiba JobinVyon, Model LabRam HR). XPS analysis of the samples was carried out in an ‘ESCALAB 250 spectrometer’ (Thermo-VG Scientific) using a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) and typical energy resolution of 0.4–0.5 eV at full width at half maximum (FWHM). The non-isothermal analysis (TGA-DTA) of the samples were carried out in a thermal analyzer (Model: Netzsch STA 449F3) in an air atmosphere at a heating rate of 5 °C/min from room temperature to 1000 °C using an Al2O3 crucible. Nitrogen sorption analysis was carried out using an AutosorbiQ Station 1, equipped with an automated surface area at 77.35 K using Brunauet-Emmett-Teller (BET) calculation for surface area. The pore size distribution plots were recorded from the desorption branch of the isotherms based on the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical study of the coal-derived GCNSs
ce:section.ce:para: A coin-like two electrode system was used for all the charge storage characteristic measurements. Electrodes for the electrochemical study were prepared by spin coating a dilute and well-sonicated dispersion of Coal-NK-S1, Coal-NK-S2 and Coal-T60-S2 samples in ethanol over graphite sheets (250 μm thick, Nickunj. Eximp. Entp. India) and dried in an oven at ∼50 °C overnight. The weight of the electrode material (∼1 mg/cm2) deposited on the graphite sheet (cell assembly was 1 cm2) was determined by weighing the graphite sheet before and after the spin coating of the electrode material. 1M H2SO4 aqueous solutions were used as an electrolyte for the device without any binder/conductive filler and two-electrode geometry was chosen for the characterization of the capacitor cells. The electrochemical characterization of the capacitor cells was performed by using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge cycling and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The CV and EIS responses were recorded with an electrochemical analyzer (660E, CH Instruments, USA).

The discharge-specific capacitance (Csp) values were estimated from the discharge characteristic regions using the expression:
(1)
C
cell
= i× Δ t/ Δ V
(2)
C
sp
= 2 ×
C
cell
/m
Where i is the constant discharge current, Δt is the discharge time interval for a voltage range ΔV, and m is the mass of single electrode material without a current collector.

The other important parameters namely, energy density and power density were evaluated by using the following expressions [38].
(3)
E =
1
2 m
C
c e l l
V 2
(4)
P =
V 2
4 M × E S R
Where C
cell is the capacitance of the Cell (in Fcm−2), V is the voltage excluding the equivalent series resistance (ESR) occurring at the beginning of the discharge profile of the Cell, and m is total mass loaded on both the electrodes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of cell for two electrode system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to construct a symmetrical configuration of an electrochemical cell, a polypropylene separator was used to soak the liquid electrolyte (1M H2SO4) sandwiched between two symmetric electrodes. All electrochemical studies were performed by using three important techniques: (1) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), (2) cyclic voltammetry (CV) and (3) galvanostatic charge-discharge cycling (GCD). The cell configurations were as follows:
Cell#1: Coal-NK-S1//Coal-NK-S1.
Cell#2: Coal-NK-S2//Coal-NK-S2.
Cell#3: Coal-T60-S2//Coal-T60-S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical characteristics of coals and coal-derived GCNSs
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates a schematic diagram for the synthesis of chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets from low-grade coal.

The physicochemical characteristics of the raw coals (i.e. Coal-NK and Coal-T60) and coal-derived GCNSs (i.e. Coal-NK-S1, Coal-NK-S2 and Coal-T60-S2) are summarized in Table 1 . Both the raw coal samples contain significant amounts of carbon and oxygen along with a small amount of hydrogen and nitrogen, which also indicate the coal samples to be of sub-bituminous rank. It was observed that the ash contents and sulfur contents of the GCNSs were lowered during both the reaction methods performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron beam analysis (SEM and TEM/HRTEM) of chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs)
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the coal-derived GCNSs was characterized by using SEM-EDX and HRTEM techniques (Fig. S1 and Fig. 1 ). After the chemical oxidation, the surface of the coal-derived GCNSs appears to be laminar shaped carbon flakes with a size range of 2–50 μm (Fig. S1a-c). The elemental composition was also characterized by energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX), and the results revealed that the chemical composition of the GCNSs formed mainly consists of carbon, oxygen, and sulfur (Fig. S1d-f). The EDX composition (wt%) of the materials was found to be 64.74% (carbon), 29.12% (oxygen), 2.36% (sulfur) for Coal-NK-S1; 45.71% (carbon), 41.67% (oxygen), 1.23% (sulfur) for Coal-NK-S2; and 50.39% (carbon), 43.88% (oxygen), 1.96% (sulfur) for Coal-T60-S2 samples. The morphologies of the GCNSs formed (Fig. 1a–b), as observed in the TEM images, clearly show the presence of chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs), may be consist of few layers of sheets [30,38,39] (See AFM images in supplementary information, Fig. S2) and it is in accordance with the results reported in the literature [30,38,39]. The presence of non-crystalline structure in the product may be due to the high oxygen content, which is also observed in the FTIR and XPS studies. Two bright ring observed in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern corresponds to the (001) and (002) crystallographic planes of chemically converted graphene-like nanosheets. The interplanar distance depends upon the concentration of the oxygen functional group on the surface of the carbon layer. The typical interplanar spacing was calculated and found to be ≃0.8 nm (001) and ≃0.5 nm (002), which is higher due to the high concentration of oxygen functional groups as compared to the graphite.
The disordered meso/micropores and carbon-channels observed (Fig. 1a–f) may provide a path for transportation of electrolyte ions as well as the occurrence of a redox reaction [5,20].

From the electron beam analysis, it was observed that both the reaction schemes (method-1 and method-2) may facilitate the formations of chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs) incorporated with some amorphous carbon from low-quality sub-bituminous coals. To further confirm the formation of graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs) from these coals, the GCNSs produced were characterized by XRD, Raman, FTIR, and XPS techniques.

Fig. 2 a shows the XRD patterns of the coal-derived GCNSs, which are similar to those, reported earlier [38]. The broad XRD peak observed at around 27.7° is due to the attribution of a graphite-like structure composed of several benzene rings (turbostratic structure) embedded with amorphous carbon matrix and the introduction of abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (mainly carboxyl and a hydroxyl group) [27,29,40,41]. However, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the coal-derived GCNSs also indicate the presence of the amorphous carbons in the samples and low impurities [42].

Fig. 2b shows the Raman spectra of the coal-derived GCNSs. The Raman spectra show mainly two characteristic bands appearing at 1595 cm−1 (G-band) and 1382 cm−1 (D-band) for Coal-NK-S1; and 1605 cm−1 (G-band) and 1387 cm−1 (D-band) for Coal-NK-S2; and1598 cm−1 (G-band) and 1374 cm−1 (D-band) for Coal-T60-S2 samples. The G-bands correspond to the first-order scattering of the E2g stretching mode of graphite and D-band is due to the residual poorly-organized graphite [27–30,38,42]. The relative integrated intensities ratio of D and G-bands (ID/IG) are estimated to be about 2.79 for Coal-NK-S1; 4.72 for Coal-NK-S2, and 3.00 for Coal-T60-S2, which may be due to the introduction of the defects to the basal planes and edges caused by the use of a strong acid during chemical oxidation (method-1 & method-2) [29,38,43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectra were recorded to investigate the surface functional groups and type of chemical bonding environment of the GCNSs (Fig. 2c). The broad absorption peaks observed at around 3639 cm−1 (Coal-NK-S1), 3381 cm−1 (Coal-NK-S2), and 3369 cm−1 (Coal-T60-S2) belongs to the O-H vibration [38,42,44–46]. The weak peaks are observed at around 2912, 2845 cm−1 (Coal-NK-S1), which may be attributed to the CH2 asymmetric vibration and CH3 symmetric vibration bonded to an aromatic group, respectively [38,42,44–46]. However, it was not observed for the GCNSs prepared in method-2. The broad peak observed at around 2541 cm−1 (Coal-NK-S1) may be attributed to the intramolecular O-H stretching [38,42,44–46] which was also not observed in the GCNSs prepared in method-2. The peaks lying in between 1600-2000 cm−1 regions are mainly due to aromatic C–H stretching, and hydrogen-bond binding quinine and carboxyl groups, and aromatic C=C stretching vibrations (G-band of graphite), respectively [38,42,44–46]. The peaks observed at around 1700 cm−1, 1600 cm−1 of the samples are mainly due to the C=O groups [29] and aromatic C=C stretching vibrations (G-band of graphite) [47]. The peak observed at around 1540 cm−1 may be attributed to the hexagonal C=C bond present in the carbon materials [38,42]. A broad peak observed at around 1440 cm−1 is mainly due to the COO− and CH3 group. A broad and intense peak observed at around 1250 cm−1 is due to the C–O stretching and O–H deformation of the carboxyl group [38,42]. The peaks at around 1034-1042 cm−1may are due to the stretching of carbohydrate or alcoholic bonds (C–O–C or C–O) [38,42]. The peaks lying in the region of 700–900 cm−1may be due to plane vibrations of aromatic C–H, benzene, and alkyl benzene bonds.

The X-ray photoelectron (XPS) analysis of the functionalization of the raw coal samples was reported in our previous study [38]. The XPS measurements of the GCNSs (Coal-NK-S1) was conducted, which indicates that the coal-derived GCNSs consists of carbon (C1S peak at 285 eV) and oxygen (O1S peak at 533 eV) (Fig. 2d). The high-resolution C1S XPS deconvoluted spectra show that the C1S XPS peak splits into three main peaks, which are located at 284.46 (C-C sp2), 285.49 (C-C sp3), and 286.77 (C-O) (Fig. 2e) [34]. The high-resolution O1S XPS fitting spectra show the presence of carboxyl (O=C-O), which are located at 531.00 eV (Fig. 2f).

Thus, the FTIR and XPS analysis revealed that the coal-derived GCNSs contain abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (i.e. carboxyl, carbonyl, and a hydroxyl group) and sp2 aromatic structure units. Furthermore, the relative FTIR peak intensity of the C=O group and C=C group of coal-derived GCNSs become stronger after the chemical oxidative reactions in method-1 and method-2, which implies a potential application for ion accumulation [48,49].

Fig. 3 a–c represents the non-isothermal Thermogravimetric analysis-Differential thermal analysis (TGA-DTA) of coal-derived graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs). The effect of heating temperature on the samples is provided in Table S1 (see supplementary information).

In TGA-DTG curve, the weight loss observed at a temperature range of 30–200 °C is for the release of water. At this stage, the total weight loss of the samples is calculated to be about 12.04% (Coal-NK-S1) (Fig. 3a), 19.70% (Coal-NK-S2) (Fig. 3b), and 28.16% (Coal-T60-S2) (Fig. 3c). The major weight losses occurred in the temperature range of 200–600 °C are due to the oxidation of carbon to CO and CO2, which is present in GCNSs [50–52]. The distinct exothermic peaks over 400 °C in the Coal-NK-S1 (two bigger and one small peak) (Fig. 3a), Coal-NK-S2 (one bigger peak) (Fig. 3b), and Coal-T60-S2 (one big peak) (Fig. 3c) were observed, in which the maximum weight loss occurred is due to the burning of aromatic as well as amorphous carbons, and it is calculated to be about 78.90%, 38.01%, and 25.58% respectively. Over 600 °C, the weight loss of the carbon materials is from the burning of aromatic as well as higher molecular weight hydrocarbon and is calculated to be about 3.97% (Coal-NK-S1), 18.22% (Coal-NK-S2), and 28.16% (Coal-T60-S2). The residual mass (carbon + ash content) of the carbon nanomaterials is calculated to be about 12.48% (Coal-NK-S1), 53.24% (Coal-NK-S2), and 63.13% (Coal-T60-S2). Moreover, the ignition temperature of the GCNSs was found to be approximately in the range of 400–500 °C, which is quite similar to the burning temperature of chemically converted graphene-like nanosheets from coal, graphene oxide, and graphene nanosheets [38]. However, the thermal stability of the Coal-NK-S1 is found to be less in comparison to that of Coal-NK-S2 and Coal-T60-S2, which may be due to the presence of high amount of oxygen-containing functional groups. A critical inspection of TG curve for Coal-NK-S2 and Coal-T60-S2 shows that the mass increases after 500 °C, which may be due to the chemisorption phenomena [53].

In summary, the above characterization results revealed the production of coal-derived chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs) containing the abundant oxygen-containing functional group, incorporated with some amorphous carbon of predominantly sp2-carbon, which is also in agreement with the earlier reports of coal-derived chemically converted graphene-like nanosheets [38] and carbon nanomaterials [27–30,42]. However, the structural difference is observed between the GCNSs, which may be due to the differences in the physicochemical properties of the raw coal and the reaction procedures [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and supercapacitor study of the coal-derived GCNSs
ce:section.ce:para:
The comparative electrochemical impedance spectra (Nyquist plots) of all the electrochemical cells with 1M H2SO4electrolyte have been performed in10 mHz–100 KHz frequency range, shown in Fig. 4
. Theoretically, for pure capacitors ideal impedance behavior is a straight line parallel to the imaginary axis Z″, whereas, in practical capacitors, the steep rising capacitive impedance response is observed in the low-frequency region accompanied with high-frequency semicircular features, owing to the bulk and interfacial properties of the capacitor cell. Such capacitive impedance response enables the evaluation of various parameters associated with the bulk properties of electrolytes and electrode-electrolyte interfaces including charge-transfer resistances, potential-dependent faradaic resistances, low-frequency capacitance values, etc., to be evaluated separately in different frequency regions. In the present case, the imaginary part of the impedance (Z″) rises steeply (almost parallel to “Z” axis) in the low-frequency region in Cell#1 electrodes, which indicates better capacitive behavior of Cell#1 than in the other two cells (Cell#2 and Cell#3), as shown in Fig. 4a. The expanded part of the impedance response in the low-frequency region (Fig. 4b) reflects as a semi-circle arc that gives the bulk properties of the cells. The values of the bulk resistance R B and interfacial charge-transfer resistance R
CT of the different capacitor cells could be easily evaluated from the intercepts on the real axis of the impedance response of supercapacitor cells for all the three different cells by drawing a semi-circular arc for corresponding cells, as shown in Fig. 4b. The values of bulk resistance (RB), charge-transfer resistance (RCT), and overall cell resistance (R) of the cells are listed in Table S2 (see supplementary information) (see Fig. 5).

The rate capability is an important aspect of consideration to design a high power supercapacitor for pulse power application, which is evaluated in term of response time (τo) performance from the EIS studies [54,55]. It has been noted that only very few studies have been performed on rate capability nature of carbon-based electrode. In the present investigation, to evaluate the rate capability of GCNSs, the response time (τo) has been evaluated by taking the reciprocal of a response frequency (f
o ) using Miller's approach [54–56]. Fig. 6 depicts the Bode plots of all Cells (Cell#1, Cell#2, Cell#3) in which response frequency estimated at the imaginary (Z″) and real (Z′) part of impedance with respect to the applied frequency (Bode plots) are found to be equal [55]. The response frequency and time for all the Cells (Cell#1, Cell#2, and Cell#3) at a phase angle of ∼45°are listed in Table S2 (see supplementary information). A lower value (∼0.196 s) of the response time (τo) has been observed for theCell#1 (Coal-NK-S1) as compared to that for the Cell#2 (Coal-NK-S2) and Cell#3 (Coal-T60-S2) which are ∼3.28 s and 3.22 s, respectively. This indicates that the electrolyte ions have faster-switching behavior across the electrode/electrolyte interface formed by Coal-NK-S1 electrodes as compared to the Coal-NK-S2 and Coal-T60-S2 electrodes. This confirms that the higher power delivering ability (discussed later) of Cell#1 (Coal-NK-S1) over the Cell#2 (Coal-NK-S2) and Cell#3 (Coal-T60-S2).

The performance of this coal-derived GCNSs as supercapacitor electrode was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement to identify the redox reaction (peaks) of the cells (Fig. 6). In the present study, the CV studies were performed at different scan rates in the potential range of −0.4 to 0.6 for Cell#1 (Coal-NK-S1) whereas the potential window of 0 V–1.0 V is used for Cell#2 (Coal-NK-S2) and Cell#3 (Coal-T60-S2). Almost rectangular CV patterns are observed at high scan rate (Fig. 6a–c) in all three cells, indicating an ideal double-layer capacitor nature of the charge-discharge process [5,57,58]. It is observed that out of these three cells, Cell#1 (Coal-NK-S1) shows mainly electric double layer (EDLC) behavior as better rectangular and large CV area is observed due to the higher conducting nature of electrode material whereas Cell#2 (Coal-NK-S2) and Cell#3 (Coal-T60-S2) show the more synergetic pseudo capacitive behavior over EDLC as the redox peak appears in CV pattern even at high scan rates (Fig. 6a–c). Pseudo and EDLC behavior of all the three cells are more clearly seen at low scan rate (5 mV s−1) as shown in Fig. 6d. It has been observed that the redox reaction i.e. oxidation and reduction hump peak in CV pattern for all the cells are due to possible reactions at a low scan rate and the separation between oxidation and reduction peaks increases as scan rate increase. Overall the voltammetry current significantly increased in Cell#1than Cell#2 and Cell#3 when employed in supercapacitor electrodes and hence from the CV study, it is found that out of the three GCNSs, Coal-NK-S1 is the promising material for supercapacitor electrode.

In order to further investigate the charge storage performance of the coal-derived GCNSs, the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) experiments were conducted in all three above-mentioned cells (Fig. 7 ). GCD cycling for Cell#1 (Fig. 7a) were performed for the potential window of −0.4–0.6 V where as for Cell#2 and Cell#3 (Fig. 7b and c) it is at 0–1.0 V at 0.5 mV s−1 for 1000 cycles. On calculating the specific capacitance (Csp) value for all three cells it was found that Cell#1 has significantly increased capacitive behavior with a specific capacitive value of∼125 F g−1 as compared to Cell#2 and Cell#3 with∼84 F g−1 and 93 F g−1, respectively. The initial fading of all three cells are low and stable after 1000 cycles while the cyclic performance is significantly improved and only ∼1–2% fading is observed in all three cases up to 1000 measured cycles as shown in Fig. 7d.

Compared with the reported data of specific capacitive value of nanomaterials, such as pure graphene (135 Fg−1) [59], pillared graphene paper electrode (138 Fg−1) [6], macroporous bubble graphene film (92.7 Fg−1) [16], reduced graphene oxide foam (110 Fg−1) [12], purified nanotubes (15–80Fg−1) [60,61], and MWNT-based supercapacitor (102 Fg−1) [62] the specific capacitive value of the as-synthesized chemically converted graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs) from low-grade coal is found to be encouraging and promising, indicating that the coal-derived GCNSs could be an alternative source for devising a supercapacitor electrode materials.

For energy and power density measurements, a charge-discharge profile has been performed at different current densities i.e. 0, 2.5, 2.0, 1.5, 1.0, 0.8 and 0.5 A g−1 for all three cells as shown in Fig. 8 a–c. The values of specific energy and power for all the cells were evaluated from equations (3) and (4) respectively, and are listed in Table 2 . A significant enhancement in the specific energy was observed for Cell#1 than Cell#2 and Cell#3, whereas the specific power density of the Cell#3 is 1128 W kg−1which is more than other two cell electrodes with values of 576 W kg−1 (Cell#1) and 772 W kg−1 (Cell#2) respectively. The variation in power and energy density for all three cells is due to their respective ESR value (sudden drop in discharge pattern) and the values are listed in Table 2. Ragone plots (i.e. power density vs. energy density) for all three cells are depicted in Fig. 8d, which shows a typical arc behavior (i.e. decrease in power density with increase in energy density) of a power source like supercapacitor [56–63].

Moreover, the single GCD cycle for all three cells is compared in Fig. 9 a. The rate capability of all cells are further analyzed by running CV curves of all three cells within their corresponding potential window at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 for 1000 cycles as shown in Fig. 9b–d, and it was found that there is no reduction in voltammetry current after 1000 CV cycles in every case. Thus, we observed that all as-synthesized GCNSs materials possess high rate capability from both CV and GCD cycling measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the specific capacitance of the GCNSs
ce:section.ce:para: The specific surface areas of the coal-derived GCNSs were determined to be 1.936 m2g−1 for Coal-NK-S1, 11.993 m2g−1 for Coal-NK-S2, and 13.276 m2g−1 for Coal-T60-S2; while pore volume were 0.049 cm3 g−1, 0.375 cm3 g−1, and 0.361 cm3 g−1, respectively. Fig. S3 (see supplementary information) shows the pore size distribution determined by N2 adsorption for the GCNSs and found to be mesoporous [64,65]. Despite their low specific surface area, these GCNSs prepared showed the high capacitance value, which is due to the presence of high oxygen-containing functional groups and mesopores [64,65].The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups (as evidenced from FT-IR and XPS analyses) and sulfur [48] (see Table 1) may enhance the wettability of GCNSs through improved pore access and greater utilization, and hence results in uniform fast charge-discharge cycles [20,22,48,64,65]. Although the specific surface area is an important parameter for the performance of a supercapacitor, but sometimes high surface area decreases the first-cycle coulombic efficiency as well as the full-cell capacity [66].

At this point, the as-prepared low-grade coal-derived GCNSs may be promising materials for supercapacitor market, which has the low surface area, high oxygen-containing functional groups, sulfur, and mesopores. However, further research is warranted in this new field for enhancement of the specific capacitance of coal-derived supercapacitor.

Abundantly available low-grade coals could be a source of supercapacitor electrode materials, which is confirmed by a multi-analytical approach of characterization consisting of chemical analysis, FT-IR, XRD, XPS, Raman, SEM-EDX, BET and HR-TEM techniques. The graphene-like carbon nanosheets (GCNSs) produced from low-grade coal was utilized as an effective electrode material for supercapacitor using an aqueous electrolyte (H2SO4 1M) with specific capacitance values of 125, 84 and 95 F g−1and high power density with high rate capability of up to 1000 GCD cycles. Both pseudo and EDLC behavior were observed in all the three cells using coal-derived GCNSs. Moreover, Cell#1 is showing better capacitance behavior as compared to Cell#2 and Cell#3. The low cost, easy and eco-friendly aspects make all three as-synthesized coal-derived GCNSs promising electrode material for supercapacitor application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to Director, CSIR-NEIST and Dr. B P Baruah for their support and encouragement during the research work. H.C. thanks UGC, India for a research fellowship. S.D. gratefully acknowledges the financial support received from University of Delhi (RC/2015/9677/882). The financial assistance from CSIR-New Delhi is duly acknowledged (MLP-6000-WP-III). Authors are acknowledged to Prof R B Finkelman for his suggestions, Dr. S.A. Hashmi and Dr. M.K. Singh for the help with electrochemistry analyses, and Dr. Lakshi Saikia for the assistance in doing AFM analyses. Authors express special thanks to the reviewers for their constructive comments to improve the revision.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Standard Practice for Preparing Coal Samples for Analysis
Standard Test Methods for Proximate Analysis of Coal and Coke by Macro Thermogravimetric Analysis
Standard Practice for Ultimate Analysis of Coal and Coke
Standard Test Method for Total Sulfur in Coal and Coke Combustion Residues Using a High-temperature Tube Furnace Combustion Method with Infrared Absorption

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J. R. Miller, In Proceedings of the Eighth International Seminar on Double Layer Capacitors and Similar Energy Storage Devices, Florida Educational Seminars, Deerfield Beach, Boca Raton, FL, Dec 7-9, 1998.
